metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `code_review.sidecar.emit()` must be restructured so that `return True` and the `prune()` call are placed OUTSIDE (after) the try/except block, matching `plan_review.sidecar.emit()` lines 63–64 exactly.
evidence: plan_review.sidecar.emit(): prune() is called on line 63, OUTSIDE the try/except, followed by `return True` on line 64
code_review.sidecar.emit(): `return True` is on line 133, INSIDE the try/except block — any statement after it in the same block is unreachable
Plan says 'matching plan-review's emit → prune sequence' without specifying that emit() must be restructured to place return True outside the try block
findings.finding: The plan instructs adding `prune(target_ticket, repo_root=repo_root)` to `code_review.sidecar.emit()` 'after append_event, matching plan-review's emit→prune sequence', but does not specify the required structural change to accommodate this: in the CURRENT `code_review.sidecar.emit()`, `return True` is inside the try/except block (line 133), making any post-return statement unreachable. Plan-review's emit places `prune()` and `return True` OUTSIDE the try/except entirely (lines 63–64). To match this sequence in code_review.emit(), the implementer must restructure by moving `return True` out of the try block — but the plan does not state this, leaving placement ambiguous (inside try before return? outside try? in its own try?).
findings.location: Scope section: 'call `prune(target_ticket, repo_root=repo_root)` from `emit()` after `append_event`, matching plan-review's emit → prune sequence' / src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py line 132–136
scenarios: An implementer adds prune() after append_event inside the existing try block, then hits `return True` — prune is never reached
An implementer adds prune() inside the try block before `return True` — a prune exception gets swallowed by the broad except and causes emit() to return False even though append_event succeeded
An implementer wraps prune in its own inner try/except inside the existing try — functionally correct but structurally inconsistent with plan-review's pattern
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'To match plan-review's emit→prune sequence, restructure `code_review.sidecar.emit()` so that `return True` moves outside the try/except block; call `prune(target_ticket, repo_root=repo_root)` on the line before `return True`, mirroring `plan_review.sidecar.emit` lines 63–64.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or tighten the existing prune oracle) requiring that the prune test exercises real git operations — e.g., on an in-process temp git repository — so the `git rm` + `git commit --no-verify` calls actually execute and the 'exactly 50 retained' assertion reflects real filesystem state, not mocked state.
evidence: AC oracle text: 'a unit test appending N>50 synthetic events on each path (via each module's prune) and asserting the retained count is exactly 50 and the newest are the ones kept'
Scope: 'git rm all but the newest keep, commit with --no-verify; best-effort and exception-swallowing'
No AC specifies that the test must exercise real git operations (a real temp repo or equivalent) rather than mocked subprocess calls
The core new behavior being defaulted into emit() is the git rm + git commit subprocess — if mocked, the suite can pass green while the live path silently no-ops or errors
findings.finding: The prune-function ACs are satisfiable by mocking `git rm` / `git commit`, which would constitute proxy-validation of the core new behavior: the actual subprocess calls (git rm all-but-newest, git commit --no-verify) are the new code path added to code_review.sidecar.emit(), yet 'unit test appending N>50 synthetic events via each module's prune' can pass green with mocked git while the live path (real git operations in a ticket branch repo) is entirely untested.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first bullet (prune behavioral oracle) and Scope section (code_review.sidecar.prune implementation)
scenarios: Tests mock subprocess/git calls; test passes; in production the git rm commits never fire (e.g., wrong branch checked out, detached HEAD, missing git binary in CI worker) — unbounded growth continues undetected
prune() catches the exception silently, emit() succeeds, reviewer sees no error but sidecars grow past 50
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the first AC to read: '…via each module's `prune` against a real (temp) git repository initialized in the test — not a mocked subprocess — so that `git rm` and `git commit --no-verify` are actually invoked and the retained count is verified against real on-disk sidecar files.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the proposed code_review prune distinguishes its own events from plan_review events in the shared per-ticket directory (e.g. by filtering on `SCHEMA` embedded in filename, a distinct EVENT_TYPE suffix, or by reading payload schema before pruning), so the two pruners cannot delete each other's REVIEW_RESULT files.
evidence: plan_review/sidecar.py line 28: EVENT_TYPE = 'REVIEW_RESULT'
code_review/sidecar.py line 21: EVENT_TYPE = 'REVIEW_RESULT'
plan_review/sidecar.py prune lines 81-85: filters by `f.endswith(f'-{EVENT_TYPE}.json')` with no schema/payload discrimination

Plan Scope says: 'add a prune(ticket_id, ...) function mirroring plan_review.sidecar.prune (list the ticket's -REVIEW_RESULT.json events by filename timestamp, git rm all but the newest keep)' — an identical filter will match both modules' events in the same directory
findings.finding: Both sidecar modules share EVENT_TYPE = 'REVIEW_RESULT' and write to the same per-ticket directory. The proposed code_review prune — which mirrors plan_review prune exactly — filters by `f.endswith(f'-{EVENT_TYPE}.json')`. This means each module's prune() will see ALL REVIEW_RESULT files in the ticket directory regardless of schema (plan-review and code-review events are indistinguishable by filename suffix alone). On a ticket that receives both plan-reviews and code-reviews, a code-review prune triggered at N>50 total REVIEW_RESULT events could delete the oldest plan-review events, and vice versa — cross-contaminating the two retention windows.
findings.location: Scope section — `code_review/sidecar.py` prune spec; compare `plan_review/sidecar.py:81-85` with `code_review/sidecar.py:21` (both EVENT_TYPE = 'REVIEW_RESULT')
scenarios: A ticket undergoes both plan-reviews and code-reviews. After 30 code-review emits, code_review prune fires and sees 30 code-review + N plan-review events in the same dir, deleting the oldest by timestamp regardless of which module wrote them.

A heavily-reviewed ticket with 40 plan-review events and 20 code-review events: the first code-review prune (keep=50) would not prune (60>50 total, but... wait — 40+20=60 total, keep=50, so 10 oldest are deleted, potentially from either module's events).
findings.suggested_fix: Give code-review sidecars a distinct filename tag (e.g. `-CODE_REVIEW_RESULT.json`) via a separate EVENT_TYPE constant, so prune filters are disjoint. Alternatively, add a schema-peek into prune() to only `git rm` files whose payload.schema matches the pruner's own SCHEMA constant. The plan-review prune already reads `f.endswith(f'-{EVENT_TYPE}.json')` — changing code_review's EVENT_TYPE to 'CODE_REVIEW_RESULT' is the lowest-blast-radius fix and avoids any payload-parse in the prune hot path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether AC #3 is a distinct test from AC #1's code-review leg (e.g., differentiate AC #3 as specifically testing the emit→prune integration path end-to-end, not just calling prune() directly) or merge AC #3 into AC #1 to eliminate the near-duplicate oracle.
evidence: AC #1: 'a unit test appending N>50 synthetic events on each path (via each module's prune) and asserting the retained count is exactly 50 and the newest are the ones kept'
AC #3: 'a test that emitting more than the cap on a code-review target ticket leaves exactly 50 events'
AC #3 adds 'via emit()' framing vs AC #1's 'via prune()' framing — but the observable outcome ('exactly 50 retained') is identical.
findings.finding: AC #1 and AC #3 have overlapping oracles for the code-review path: AC #1 already specifies 'a unit test appending N>50 synthetic events on each path (via each module's prune) and asserting the retained count is exactly 50' (covering code-review), and AC #3 separately specifies 'a test that emitting more than the cap on a code-review target ticket leaves exactly 50 events' — these are nearly identical tests for the same behavior.
scenarios: Test authors write two nearly identical tests satisfying both ACs, creating maintenance duplication.
One oracle is written to satisfy both, leaving ambiguity about which AC is 'done'.
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC #3 to sharpen its distinction: 'Oracle: an integration-style test calling code_review.sidecar.emit() (not prune() directly) more than 50 times and asserting exactly 50 REVIEW_RESULT files remain, confirming the emit→prune wiring (as opposed to AC #1 which tests prune() directly on synthetic files).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (or enforce) that concurrent emit() calls for the same ticket_id are serialized (e.g., via a per-ticket file lock, the caller's existing serialization, or explicit acknowledgement that the race is accepted and why it is safe), so the TOCTOU between list and git-rm is addressed.
evidence: No locking or serialization mechanism is mentioned for concurrent prune operations on the same ticket
The sequence 'list → git rm → commit' is a classic check-then-act pattern with no atomicity guarantee
Exceptions are swallowed, so the second concurrent caller silently fails if git rm targets already-removed files, leaving event count potentially above the cap
findings.finding: The emit→prune sequence performs a non-atomic list-then-delete-then-commit on the shared tickets branch, creating a TOCTOU race when two concurrent emit() calls target the same ticket_id — both list the same files, both compute the same deletion set, and the second git-rm/commit can conflict or operate on already-deleted files.
findings.location: Scope section — code_review.sidecar: 'list the ticket's -REVIEW_RESULT.json events by filename timestamp, git rm all but the newest keep, commit with --no-verify'
scenarios: Two drift-refresh reviews are triggered simultaneously on the same ticket; both emit() calls race through prune(); one succeeds, the other's git rm fails silently leaving more than 50 events
At-least-once delivery retries an emit() while a previous attempt's prune commit is in flight; git commit conflicts are swallowed, leaving the branch in a partially-pruned state

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit recovery note to the rollback section stating that pruned code-review events are recoverable from git history (e.g., 'git log --diff-filter=D -- <ticket>-REVIEW_RESULT.json' to identify and restore deleted files) so the back-out path is concrete, not just implied.
evidence: 'Code-review sidecars that already grew unbounded will be pruned to 50 on the next emit.'
'no migration' — rollback section describes only future-state behavior after revert, not how to recover data destroyed by the first emit after deploy.
T4 criterion: 'The remedy is an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract — acknowledgement alone is insufficient.'
findings.finding: The first post-deploy emit on the code-review path will irrecoverably prune existing unbounded REVIEW_RESULT events to 50. The plan acknowledges this ('will be pruned to 50 on the next emit') but provides no explicit rollback or recovery mechanism — T4 requires more than acknowledgement: a concrete back-out path (e.g., 'git history preserves pruned events; to recover, cherry-pick the deleted files from the pre-prune commit') must be stated.
scenarios: An operator deploys this change; one code-review emit fires and prunes events 51-N; the operator then decides to roll back — the pruned events are gone from the working tree with no stated recovery path.
An auditor realizes post-deploy they needed event #47 that was pruned; no recovery procedure is documented.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Compatibility & rollback: 'Pruned code-review REVIEW_RESULT events are deleted via `git rm` and committed, so they remain recoverable from git history. To recover: `git log --all --diff-filter=D -- "*<ticket>*REVIEW_RESULT.json"` identifies the deletion commit; `git checkout <commit>^1 -- <path>` restores individual files. This is the rollback path for already-pruned events; reverting the constant/code only prevents future pruning.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50` to a neutral shared module (e.g., `rebar/llm/sidecar_constants.py` or `rebar/llm/review_constants.py`) and have both `plan_review.sidecar` and `code_review.sidecar` import from it, so neither sibling depends on the other.
evidence: Plan states: 'reference the shared bound (import RETAIN_PER_TICKET from rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar, so there is ONE named constant governing both paths)'
plan_review and code_review are sibling packages; no stated reason why plan_review should be the authority over a constant that governs both
findings.finding: The plan routes the shared constant through an asymmetric module dependency: `code_review.sidecar` imports `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` from `plan_review.sidecar`. This makes `code_review` a downstream dependent of `plan_review` with no logical justification for that direction — if `plan_review` is ever renamed, split, or removed, `code_review` silently breaks. A neutral shared location (e.g., `rebar.llm.sidecar_constants` or `rebar.llm.constants`) would own the constant and both sibling modules would import from there, preserving the single-definition goal without the directional coupling.
scenarios: plan_review module is refactored/renamed in a future story, silently breaking code_review's import
A developer adding a third review type must import from plan_review, further entrenching the asymmetric dependency
Linters or architecture checks that enforce sibling-module isolation flag the import
findings.suggested_fix: Create `src/rebar/llm/review_constants.py` containing `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50`. Update `plan_review/sidecar.py` to `from rebar.llm.review_constants import RETAIN_PER_TICKET` (removing the local definition). Update `code_review/sidecar.py` to import from the same location. AC oracle test asserts both modules' `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` equal 50 and trace back to the same object (`plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a `log.warning` (or equivalent structured log) inside the exception-swallowing block of the new code-review `prune()` so operators can detect and debug silent prune failures.
evidence: Plan states prune is 'best-effort and exception-swallowing' but specifies no log.warning/log.error or counter increment on the caught exception
An external dep (git) going down/slow would cause every prune to silently fail with no operator signal
plan_review.sidecar's existing prune behaviour is not confirmed to log either, but the new code-review prune path is the new failure point being introduced here
findings.finding: The new code-review prune path swallows exceptions silently with no logging or metric, so operators cannot detect when prune consistently fails and code-review REVIEW_RESULT events accumulate unboundedly again — defeating the purpose of the cap.
findings.location: Scope section — code_review.sidecar: 'best-effort and exception-swallowing — a failed prune never fails the gate'
scenarios: git is unavailable or the working tree is locked; every emit silently skips pruning; unbounded growth resumes with no alert
A bug in the filename-timestamp parsing causes every prune to raise; the cap is silently never enforced
findings.suggested_fix: In the except block of code_review.sidecar.prune(), add: `logger.warning('code_review sidecar prune failed for ticket %s: %s', ticket_id, exc)` before continuing, mirroring whatever observability plan_review.sidecar.prune already has.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconsider the cross-module import of `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` from `plan_review.sidecar` into `code_review.sidecar`: the established pattern across all three peer sidecars (`completion_sidecar`, `digest_sidecar`, `plan_review/sidecar`) is each defines its own constant independently, and `completion_sidecar.py`'s untouched `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 10` already undermines the 'no second literal' rationale — either extend the import to ALL peer sidecars (completing the abstraction) or simply define `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50` locally in `code_review/sidecar.py` (matching the codebase convention).
evidence: src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py:34: RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 10  — independent definition, not updated by this plan
src/rebar/llm/overlap/digest_sidecar.py:39: RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 1  — independent definition
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:43: RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 10  — each existing sidecar defines its own constant; none import from a peer

Plan Approach rationale: 'A single shared constant (imported, not duplicated) keeps the change minimal and makes "governs both paths" literally true' — but completion_sidecar is a third path with the same literal value that is not brought under this governance
Rule-of-Three fails: only 1 module (code_review.sidecar) would import from plan_review.sidecar; 2 peer sidecars continue with local definitions
findings.finding: The cross-module import of RETAIN_PER_TICKET from plan_review.sidecar into code_review.sidecar is a premature abstraction: it has exactly 1 call-site, while the established codebase pattern (completion_sidecar.py, digest_sidecar.py, plan_review/sidecar.py) is each sidecar defines its own constant independently. The plan's 'no second literal to drift' rationale is immediately undercut by completion_sidecar.py, which also holds RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 10 and is not updated — leaving a counter-example that invalidates the single-source-of-truth claim.
findings.location: Scope section / Approach rationale — 'reference the shared bound (import RETAIN_PER_TICKET from rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar, so there is ONE named constant governing both paths — no second literal to drift)'
scenarios: The 'no drift' rationale fails immediately: if RETAIN_PER_TICKET is later changed in plan_review.sidecar, completion_sidecar.py's independent copy still drifts — the abstraction is half-applied.

The directional cross-component import (code_review depends on plan_review) introduces a real coupling for a single integer constant, which can cause confusion and is inconsistent with the module's existing practice of importing only utilities (norm_id) rather than configuration.
findings.suggested_fix: Define `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50` directly in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` (matching the existing pattern in `completion_sidecar.py` and `digest_sidecar.py`). If a truly shared constant is desired, extract it to a neutral shared location (e.g., a new `rebar.llm.sidecar_constants` module) and update ALL three peer sidecars — but that is a larger refactor that should be its own story.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the Approach Rationale that `completion_sidecar.py` has its own independent `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 10` for a different event type (`COMPLETION_VERDICT`), and clarify that the 'no second literal' claim applies only to the `REVIEW_RESULT` paths in scope (plan_review + code_review), not to the completion sidecar.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py line 34: RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 10 (independent literal, not imported from plan_review.sidecar)
Plan Approach Rationale: 'A single shared constant (imported, not duplicated) keeps the change minimal and makes "governs both paths" literally true — no new config key (YAGNI...)'
The plan names only two files in Scope; completion_sidecar.py is not mentioned
findings.finding: src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py has its own independent RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 10 literal (confirmed in the file). The plan's Approach Rationale says the shared import creates 'literally true' that there is 'no second literal to drift,' but after this change a third independent literal remains in completion_sidecar.py. The plan neither enumerates this file as an intentionally-excluded sibling nor adjusts the 'no second literal' rationale to acknowledge it.
findings.location: Scope section / Approach rationale — 'no second literal to drift'
scenarios: A future maintainer raising the cap again may consult the plan's 'single constant' rationale and miss that completion_sidecar.py still has an independent literal at the old value, causing the two caps to drift.

A reviewer auditing 'one constant governs all REVIEW_RESULT retention' finds completion_sidecar.py governs COMPLETION_VERDICT (a different event type), so the drift concern is lower — but the plan's language overstates universality.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to Approach Rationale: 'Note: `completion_sidecar.py` has its own independent `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 10` governing `COMPLETION_VERDICT` events (a different event type, a different prune path); this story does not touch it. The "no second literal" claim applies specifically to the two `REVIEW_RESULT` paths (plan_review and code_review).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Realign the leaf to be a strict subset of parent db7b: either (a) remove the code_review/sidecar.py deliverables from this leaf and file a separate story under the correct b744/WS4 parent lineage to add code-review retention; or (b) update parent db7b's plan to explicitly include the code-review prune path, which stales db7b's plan-review attestation and requires db7b to be re-reviewed before this leaf is re-reviewed.
evidence: Parent db7b §Scope: 'Retention is instead a dedicated PRUNE (rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.prune) that keeps the most-recent RETAIN_PER_TICKET sidecars per ticket' — scoped exclusively to plan_review.sidecar

Parent db7b AC4: 'A retention/cleanup path bounds sidecar growth … a dedicated sidecar.prune (keep most-recent RETAIN_PER_TICKET per ticket, remove older), run best-effort after emit — NOT compaction' — no mention of code_review/sidecar.py

Parent db7b §What: 'A reducer-IGNORED REVIEW_RESULT event capturing per-criterion verdicts + finding fingerprints + metadata + override justifications' — describes plan-review observability substrate only
src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py line 1: 'Code-review verdict sidecar (epic b744 / WS4)' — belongs to a different parent lineage (b744/WS4) than db7b (child of 5fd2)
Leaf §Current state acknowledges code_review/sidecar.py 'has NEITHER a prune() function NOR a RETAIN_PER_TICKET constant' — confirming this is a new, net-new addition not covered by db7b's scope
findings.finding: The leaf delivers a second scope item — adding prune() and RETAIN_PER_TICKET to code_review/sidecar.py — that is entirely outside its parent db7b's declared scope. Parent db7b's What, Scope, and all ACs reference only rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.prune and never mention code_review/sidecar.py, the code-review sidecar, or any code-review retention path. code_review/sidecar.py itself declares it belongs to epic b744/WS4 — a completely different parent lineage from db7b (child of epic 5fd2). The parent WINS: the leaf may not deliver something outside the parent's plan without first updating the parent.
findings.location: Leaf §Scope (code_review/sidecar.py bullets); leaf §Current state; parent db7b §What, §Scope, and AC4
scenarios: An implementer following db7b's scope contract expects changes only to src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py; this leaf also modifies code_review/sidecar.py, which is in a different epic's ownership lane, surprising reviewers auditing against db7b as the authoritative contract.

If the code-review prune addition has a bug or test failure, it is unclear which story's remediation loop owns the fix — the parent (db7b, now closed) has no awareness of this deliverable, and the correct b744/WS4 story was never updated to include it.

A future code-review retention change will find an orphaned prune() with a cross-epic import dependency (code_review importing from plan_review) with no owning parent story in the b744/WS4 lineage, making architectural bookkeeping difficult.
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt option (a): strip the code_review/sidecar.py scope items (all bullets in §Scope referencing code_review/sidecar.py, the 'ADDS an equivalent prune path to code-review' language in §Current state, ACs 1/2/3 sub-clauses referencing code-review) from this leaf. File a new story under epic b744/WS4 to add code-review retention governed by the shared constant. The remaining leaf — raise RETAIN_PER_TICKET from 10→50 in plan_review/sidecar.py — is a clean subset of db7b AC4.

findings.finding: The Scope section specifies `prune(..., keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET)` as a Python default parameter, which binds the value at function-definition time. AC2(c) then requires a monkeypatch of the single constant to make BOTH prune paths retain N — but monkeypatching a module-level name after import does NOT retroactively change an already-bound default parameter in Python, so the monkeypatch test described in AC2 would fail with the implementation approach described in Scope.
findings.location: Scope (prune signature) vs. Acceptance Criteria AC2(c)
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope, change the prune signature to `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=None, repo_root=None)` and resolve `keep` inside the body as `keep = keep if keep is not None else plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET`. This way the constant is read at call time, the default-parameter object-identity test (AC2a/b) still holds for the `None` sentinel, and the monkeypatch test (AC2c) works correctly. Alternatively, rewrite AC2(c) to patch via a module reload.

findings.finding: The plan mandates `prune(..., keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET)` as a Python default parameter (Scope section) while simultaneously requiring AC2(c) to monkeypatch the single module-level constant and assert both prune paths honor the patched value. Python binds default argument values at function-definition time, so patching `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` AFTER the function is defined does not change the already-captured default. AC2(c) as written is mechanically unachievable with the default-parameter design AC2(b) and Scope mandate — the monkeypatch test will always see the original bound value (50), not the patched small N.
findings.location: Scope section (`prune(..., keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET, ...)`) and AC2(c) (`monkeypatches the single constant … asserts BOTH prune paths then retain N`)
findings.suggested_fix: Change the prune signature to `def prune(ticket_id, *, keep=None, repo_root=None)` and resolve `keep = RETAIN_PER_TICKET if keep is None else keep` inside the function body. This makes the module-level constant read at call time, so AC2(c)'s monkeypatch works correctly. Update AC2(b) to check `prune.__defaults__` or the sentinel pattern accordingly.

findings.finding: AC2's monkeypatch oracle (sub-clause c) is structurally incompatible with the import strategy specified in Scope. The plan says `from rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar import RETAIN_PER_TICKET` and then `def prune(..., keep: int = RETAIN_PER_TICKET, ...)`. In Python, a default-parameter value is captured at function-definition time as the integer object then bound to that name — not re-read from `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` at call time. After monkeypatching `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET = N`, `code_review.sidecar.prune`'s default `keep` is still the old integer object, so AC2(c)'s assertion that 'both prune paths then retain N' will fail. The import + default-parameter pattern cannot satisfy the late-binding semantic the test oracle requires.
findings.location: Scope section (code_review.sidecar — import + prune signature) and AC2 (sub-clauses b and c)
findings.suggested_fix: In code_review.sidecar, use a module-level re-export: `from rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar import RETAIN_PER_TICKET` (satisfies the `is` check at module level for AC2a). Define prune with a `None` sentinel default: `def prune(ticket_id, *, keep=None, repo_root=None): if keep is None: from rebar.llm.plan_review import sidecar as _pr; keep = _pr.RETAIN_PER_TICKET`. Update AC2(b) to assert the MODULE attribute identity (`code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET`) rather than the function default, and AC2(c) works because the monkeypatched value is read lazily at call time.

findings.finding: AC2(c)'s monkeypatch oracle is non-functional as specified: Python captures the default-parameter value at function-definition time, so monkeypatching `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` after import does NOT change the `keep` default of either `prune()` function. A test calling `prune()` with no args after monkeypatching will still use the old value (50), causing the test to assert the wrong thing or fail silently as a no-op validator — a self-authored-oracle risk that locks in the impression of live-constant-reading without actually proving it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2(c): "monkeypatches the single constant to a small N and asserts BOTH prune paths then retain N"
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, change `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET, repo_root=None)` to use a `None` sentinel: `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=None, repo_root=None)` with `if keep is None: keep = RETAIN_PER_TICKET` inside the body. This makes the constant read at call time, so a monkeypatch test (set `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 5`, call both `prune()` with no args, assert 5 retained) will actually work as intended. Update AC2 to match.

findings.finding: The AC2 oracle uses Python `is` (object identity) to verify that code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is the imported constant rather than a copied literal, but CPython interns all small integers (−5 to 256), so a copied `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50` in code_review/sidecar.py would also satisfy `code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` — the oracle cannot distinguish an import from a copied literal at this value, defeating its stated purpose.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 oracle: 'asserts `code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` (OBJECT IDENTITY, so a copied literal that merely equals 50 fails)'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `is` sub-assertion from AC2 (it cannot distinguish import from copied literal for values ≤ 256) and rely on AC2(c)'s monkeypatch test as the sole oracle for the single-constant invariant: monkeypatching `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` to a small N and asserting both prune paths then retain N proves both paths read the one constant at call time, which a copied literal would fail.

findings.finding: AC2's stated oracle ('a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value') is weaker than AC2's stated criterion ('The cap is one named constant governing both paths'). A mirrored-constants implementation (two separate variables both set to 50) satisfies the oracle but violates the 'one named constant' structural claim, meaning the AC can be green while the criterion is false.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'The cap is one named constant governing both plan- and code-review prune paths. Oracle: a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value.'
findings.suggested_fix: AC2 oracle: 'a test asserting `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` (import identity), confirming a single definition site; plus `grep -c "RETAIN_PER_TICKET\s*=" src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` should equal 1 (only one assignment exists in the codebase).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
